sentences.text: No, the-- there were two other boys, from our area, from Velike Loucky-- it was called Nagy Lucska during the Hungarians-- that were called in at the same time as I was.
And matter of fact, I had a cousin there, from [NON-ENGLISH], also a Katz boy.
And all-- all-- all the kids that were in that camp were 21 years old.
I mean, they were called in at the same time.
Oh, yes.
There was nice friendship between us.
Well, they-- they were disciplinarians.
But it wasn't anything out of ordinary that you need to pay attention.
No, they were-- they were friendly.
Well, yes.
They were-- they-- they were trying to scare us.
They were having fun.
They piled us up.
They-- they made us go on a-- on a pile of garbage and tamp down the garbage and sing Hungarian songs, that they were directed.
I mean, they were just having a lot of fun with us.
That was a couple of days before the march from Budapest, that they were taking us into Germany.
I would like to point out, at this time, that my mother was taken away in April.
But June, I received a postcard from her in camp, while I was in Budakalasz, telling me that they are doing well, not to worry about them.
Which, now I know-- which now I know that they made them before they killed them.

They made them write a card, to anybody that they had left at home, which served a double purpose, to find out whether anybody was hiding and, also, to confuse anybody that received the card, to find out and, you know, to let us know that they are-- they are doing well.
The card-- excuse me.
The card told me that she's doing well, not to worry.
And hopefully, we will be able to see each other soon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

Well, this thought, that my mother is there, in Germany, made me feel that I feel-- I felt like going into Germany to find out what happened to my friends, what happened to my girlfriend, Etta, who is my wife, and my parents, and their parents, and what happened to any of the Jews that took-- they took away from home.
We were on a march from Budapest to Hegyeshalom.
Hegyeshalom was the town bordering the Austrian border.
I know you would like me to spell Hegyeshalom, so I'll spell it for you.
It's H-E-G-Y-E-S capital H-O-L-M-- H-O-L-O-M.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
On that march, we encountered-- [SIGHS] I have a blank-- blank in my name.
What was his name?
From Sweden, the diplomat?
Wallenberg?
Yeah, Wallenberg was handing out papers, Swedish citizenship papers.
Now any of the kids that received-- that got those papers were not let in into Germany.
They were denied entry into Germany by the border.
So he saved a lot of Jews.
I did not want to take any paper from him, because I wanted to go into Germany.
And the reason I wanted to go into Germany is to find out how my mother is doing, how everybody is doing.
As a matter of fact, I worked in the office.

And we were-- and a day before they turned us over to the Germans, at the border, we were working on legal documents made out between the Hungarian government and the German government, listing everybody that was turned over to Germany.
That the-- the Hungarian government is turning over x amount of workers to the German government.
They are of Jewish faith.
And these people-- and the German government is going to be responsible for them, to-- to bring them back, so forth and so on.
I mean, it was a formality.
Those-- those orders were worked in the office.
And I, working in the office, I was helping with the papers, with the list of-- of the-- the list of prisoners that we're going to be turned over to the German government.
We didn't realize-- we didn't realize how bad it is until we got into the German border.
Because, on the Hungarian side, they were still feeding us.
We were still housed properly.
The next day, when they turned us over, over the border, when they brought us over the border, and at the train station the-- it was snowing.
It was very cold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somehow, from sunshine in Hungary, it went into-- to freezing weather into Germany.
The SS were youngsters.
They were-- they were young, like young kids, 13-, 14-year-old kids with uniforms.
And they saw what was-- what the SS were doing to us, they were standing with tears in their eyes.
I mean, they-- they couldn't comprehend what is happening.
And at-- at that time, they put us on trains.
And the train had compartments, two benches on each side, a door leading into the compartment, with a riser on the front of the train.
I think there were 10 of us, in each side, on each bench, sitting in a compartment, about 20 of us in 1 compartment.
The doors were closed.
Somehow, we were all hungry and thirsty.
One of the-- one of our-- one of the guys in the compartment pushed down the window to get some snow to quench his thirst.
And an SS saw and-- what, you know, that he opened a window.
He got on the riser.
He pushed down the window and took the machine gun and was just shooting.
The guy on the left of me got it in the mouth, and then the right of me got it in the mouth.
I survived.
Half of the people were killed that were on that train.
That was the first-- first experience in-- coming into Germany.
Then the train--
Nothing.
Nothing.
They just laid on the ground until we got to Flossenbuerg.
They were there.
We didn't have any food.
The-- the concentration camp that they took us-- us was Flossenbuerg.
We got into Flossenbuerg to the station.
It was late at night.
We still had our clothes.
We still had-- I still had my locker, with me, that I was carrying.
The SS-- the SS were-- started yelling, raus, raus, get off the train, get off the train.
And we got off the train.
And we were marching into the camp.
And it was a beautiful sight, because, as we were marching in the dark, we saw a hill lit up with lights.
Later, we realized that it was Flossenbuerg concentration camp.
We came in through the gate, the lights-- the lights were shining in our eyes.
And the big SS were standing there, with a-- with a glow on their face, looking at us.
As a matter of fact, even till this day-- I live here in Mission Hills-- they have a light by the gate at night.
And when I drive in at night, many times, it hits me that the same light that at Flossenbuerg shined in my eyes.
And those are the memories that we are living it-- that I am living with.
Got into Flossenbuerg.
They put us in a big assembly hall.
I had a top coat, that I was very--
Yeah, go ahead.
Go ahead.
Right.
From Budapest?
I don't know.
It must-- must have taken about three, four days.
And--
No.
No.
But at the train?
No.

We-- I don't remember any discussions, but the-- the-- the people that were walking from Budapest, we encountered a group of people that were brought in from-- also, I think kids that were two years or three years older than us, that were in the munkaszolgalat in-- in Yugoslavia.
And those-- you couldn't understand it.
Those people were raggedy.
They were in women's clothing.
They were like animals.
They were hungry.
They were starved.
They were begging.
They were-- their eyes were bulging.
I mean, it was absolutely different.
You couldn't understand what happened to those people, I mean, why they were so haunted.
But they endured a lot of hardship.
They were under German command.
And they were marching them from Yugoslavia to the same border as we were on.
[SIGHS] Well, at night, when we came in, we didn't see any of the-- any makeup of the camp.
But they herded us into a big-- big, empty hall.
And they told us to rest up.
And the next day, they are going to take us to a shower.
And we should put our clothes in order, and-- because we are going to get back to them.
And we should have-- we have to recognize the clothes when we come back.
The next morning, everybody had their clothes in order.
As a matter of fact, I had a top coat.
I was very concerned that it shouldn't get wrinkled.
So I rolled it up, very carefully, to sleep on it.
It was a-- it was a stone floor that we were sleeping on.
And the next morning, they made us undress.
They took us to the showers.
And that was the end of our clothing.
And this is the end of connection with the past.
The SS.
You know, and I'll tell you something, the SS also had helpers from people in the camp, themselves.
Those were called, the-- oh, my, I forgot their names.
It'll come to me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I-- I-- I cannot think of their names.
But--
Kapos, yeah.
Those were the kapos that were helping.
They were helping the Germans.
And they were beating to prove to the-- they were beating us to prove to the Germans that they are-- they are very serious in their jobs.
And the next day, like I said, they took us to the showers.
No-- no water came out.
But they made us undress.
And we came in there.
And we walked out of there.
And they-- then they fed us.
And we were-- we were at Flossenbuerg about three or four weeks without doing anything.
They had-- the trainload of people that we came in was-- was situated in two barracks.
The ones that were-- they-- they were healthy, like myself, were in the barracks on one side.
And the other side, they were sick, sick in the barracks.
And those people-- it was just accumulating dead people every morning.
And they carried them away.
They were-- died from starvation and from sicknesses.
The makeup of the camp of Flossenbuerg was, there were a lot of German prisoners there, a lot of homosexuals.
And they were recognized by the triangular square that they were wearing.
I think it was a pink square that they wore.
And they had their numbers.
They had striped clothing.
They were very well dressed.
They were very-- they-- they-- they looked good.
They looked healthy.
And looking at those people, we figured that we had something to live for, that there's hope.
You know, they're not going to torture us.
Except, we-- at that time, we didn't realize that they were going to make an exception between those people and the Jews.
They also had a house of prostitution in the camp, for the German officers and for the prisoners that were helping to run the camp.
I have no idea.
I never saw anybody.
Just-- it was just pointed out that that was the house of prostitution.
The-- from Flossenbuerg, we didn't do any work in Flossenbuerg until they took us away.
And they took us to Ohrdruf, which was a concentration camp.
Not-- I don't know how far it was to Buchenwald-- from Buchenwald.
But it was connected to Buchenwald.
Yes, there were a lot of punishments.
A matter of fact, at that time, the people that were-- that were try to assassinate Hitler was-- was hung in Flossenbuerg.
We saw some hangings in there.
And we had to assemble in the hall.
We had to witness the-- those hangings.
And people were beaten.
People were punished.
For no reason at all.
At-- at-- at that time, we were not working yet.
We didn't go out.
It just-- in the morning, it was appel.
We had to go outside and-- for the count of the people, how many people are in there.
And they made us stand without any-- without any warm clothing for two to three hours, until the German officers came out, and they counted us.
And they were satisfied that nobody was missing.
And then they put us back into the barracks.
And we-- and they-- we spent the day in the barracks.
And then, in the evening, they had another appel and just going on and on.
No, not in a sense, because I was trying to avoid-- to avoid contact or to do something that was displeasing any of the officers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not-- nothing that I can point out as far as punishment is concerned, until we got to Ohrdruf, which was the camp that we were supposed-- that we started working.
And Ohrdruf, again, we were in the barracks.
We were sleeping, eight of us in a cot.
In a-- that was layered cots, that we were eight of us, that were-- that was built for two, actually.
And if anybody's head was sticking out at night, they beat it in.
So we had to be very careful not to overhang the sides, to be pushed-- pull together.
A lot of people that were sick died.
They-- they died in their sleep.
And in the morning, they picked them up, and they-- they discarded them.
The inmates, in Ohrdruf, were mostly Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The work that we went out to was the-- we were working in stone quarries the-- in the hills.
I think they were building, making room for those V8 engines that they were shooting over into England.
But it was day-- day shift, and there was a night shift.
And I remember the first time they took us out to work at night, we were standing outside, waiting for an hour, a couple of hours, for the trucks to pick us up.
I will never forget.
It started raining, very hard.
And I says, oh, my gosh, it's beautiful.
Now they're not going to take us to work.
I was mistaken.
We were working in the rain, on the outside, all night.
And without any clothing, It was very, very hard.
It was very, very cold.
It was bad.
The bosses on the camp, the inside of the camp, the SS did not come into the inside of the camp.
There was-- the-- the-- the camp was run by the SS from the outside.
Only the German officers came in for appel to count the prisoners.
But the SS guards were on the outside of the perimeter of the camp.
The kapos were running the camp, themselves, helping the Germans.

During the-- I know, during the day, when-- I mean, during the mornings, when we-- after the appels, when we-- they were assembled to go to work, they had bands playing, on the entry-- at the exit ways.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They made it-- they made it look very festive.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very hard on us.
Food, we didn't get any food in the morning.
We got a little soup or-- without-- without anything in it.
This was our sustenance for the day.
They gave us a little slice of bread, that we finished immediately, because, if we didn't finish it, somebody stole it from us.
And so that was-- that was the whole thing.
That was day in and day out.
And we were just going to work.

And if somebody-- if-- if at work, we saw any food that we picked up, if we worked in the fields and brought it into the camp, and they found the-- the-- the turnips or any kind of food on us, they were punished severely.
Coming into camp, they had people tied up by the posts, with their-- hanging from the posts, from the back.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were all kinds.
The had all kinds of punishment for all kinds of-- for-- for us.
No.
Not that I know of, nobody escaped.
The only one that I know, that a friend, a friend of mine, who was from Velike Loucky, from Nagy Lucska, at the same camp, he was two years older.
And when we were working on those-- we were working in those stone quarries, somehow, he wandered off to the side.
And the SS thought that he wants to escape.
And he shot him in the leg.
And at night, when after work, when they finished, and we-- they put us on the buses to take back to camp, they were trampling on him.
Because they threw him on the ground.
And I prevailed on an SS should put him aside.
And he put him aside.
He-- I know, when we went-- when we went back to camp, he was put into the sick barracks.
And he had a cousin that was taking care of him.
But once those sick barracks filled up to capacity, they emptied them out.
They told him they're taking him to rehabilitation.
And that was the end.
We know now where they were taking them.
As a matter of fact, the SS used to come into camp and talk to us and say to us, we know you're working very hard.
You're not getting enough food.
Anybody that feels, that needs a little rest, please volunteer, please tell us.
We are going to take you for-- we're going to take you to a rehabilitation center, for three, four weeks, and then we'll bring you back.
And the kids volunteered.
As a matter of fact, they were running after the trucks, take me, take me, take me.
You left me behind.
And they took them straight to the crematorium.
Ohrdruf, right?
[SIGHS] Not really.
At that point, no.
The-- everybody was treated the same, with the same misery.
I mean, the kapos were treating everybody alike.
There was no difference.
The kapos, some of them were Jewish.
Some of them were Polacks.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's all, really.
Yes.
I think Wagner's.
Wagner's, the Wagner music.
They played march songs, marching songs.
And they take-- took us out of the camp.
And the SS also liked to listen to some of the-- some of the arias, opera arias.
And there were some prisoners that were singers, or they were performers from the opera.
They used to come into the barracks.
If they found out that there's somebody that knows how to sing, they used to give them a piece of bread, and they were listening to the-- they were enjoying the music.
We were-- we were all carted up in the beds.
And the-- the- couple of-- couple of people that were professional, from opera singers, were-- were doing some performance for them.
No religious life in the camp that I know of.
We didn't know what day it was on Earth.
We had absolutely no idea where we were.
And I mean, what-- what day of the-- of the week it is at all.
Oh, the Hungarian people talked about cooking, cooking, how they liked to bake cakes, and how they liked to cook.
And they were-- and they were starving.
And they were-- they were-- It was unreal.
They were talking between themselves.
And keep on cooking, and-- and-- and-- and-- and their mouth was watering and dropping.
No, just the immediate people between ourselves.
Everybody was taking care of themselves and trying to keep-- trying to-- to follow the orders of the kapos or of the SS.
When they-- when they woke you up in the morning, you were trying to get up in a hurry.
They-- you shouldn't have to be beaten.
Or if you lagging, you were beat-- being beaten with sticks.
And to standing outside, and we were just huddling and moving between ourselves to keep warm.
No, we didn't hear any news of the war at all.
We didn't know how the war was going.
Rumors were in camp that the war is coming to an end.
We were having hope.
But nothing-- nothing, really.
We really didn't know what was going on outside.
Well, the food distribution was only once a day, that they gave you in the morning, or in the evening, the soup, the thin soup, which is very, very watery, and a slice of bread.
And that's what you ate.
And that's what you kept the sustenance.
If people-- people towards-- towards the-- when you were there already a couple of-- a few weeks, and you were weakened from the-- from the labor, and people got sick, they got diarrhea.
They recognized that they have to help themselves, because if they-- if they report for sick leave, they take you into the-- to the-- to the barracks.
And they let you die.
They didn't even give you any food there.
So they were taking the bread, and they had a stove in-- in the barracks.
And they were burning it, making charcoal out of it and healed-- healed themselves that way.
Did you ever use food in exchange for anything else?
Or any other object in exchange for food?
Not myself, no.
Never had a chance.
Never had to-- to do anything like that.
Well, the end of Ohrdruf, we were working there.
And they evidently, when they-- either the Russians or the Americans were coming close.
They were trying to empty the camp.
And at that time, we were-- they had us on a death march from Ohrdruf to Buchenwald.
I don't know how far it was.
And I don't remember how many days.

But it was a death march to the-- to the effect that anybody that couldn't keep up with the march, or anybody that felt tired and sat down by the side of the road, got a bullet in the head, immediately.
And we were pushing ourselves to get into the camp.
We finally got to Buchenwald.
And we were-- there was no room for us in Buchenwald.
So we were outside, on the square, sitting and waiting for orders, what would happen to us.
The next day, orders came that we are-- they are taking us out of the camp.
And at that time, when they were taking us out of the camp, we already saw the American people, the American planes overhead dropping food packages.
They, at that time, they already knew that they are-- they have encountered a concentration camp.
And now, I know that a day after they took us out of that camp, they liberated the camp.
In Ohrdruf?
[SIGHS] From, let's say, January [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till the-- till April, until the end of March.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we had a death-- the death march from Ohrdruf to Buchenwald.
Then in--
That was about three weeks before the end of the war, before we were liberated, which was April.
They load us-- loaded us on a train, about, I think it was 1,200 or 1,400.
Everybody got a full loaf of bread.
And we were very happy about it.
Because we could devour the bread immediately.
And I know-- I know for a fact, that three weeks later, when we were liberated, we had no food.
Because they didn't feed us at all.
If the train stopped someplace, at-- at a clearing, they let us off the train.
They surrounded with SS.
The people got off the train.
And the-- the grass and the leaves from the trees disappeared immediately.
Because we ate up the grass.
We ate up the leaves, whichever, whatever was edible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At that point, at that point, starvation was very, very great, because we had absolutely no food for three weeks.
They didn't give us any-- any food in the transport.
And the Germans, the German SS were delighted in torturing us by having cooked potatoes and peeling the potatoes and dropping them on the ground.
And anybody that could jump on that peeling to devour, to eat it up.
People were like animals.
People would-- people were eating-- cutting off-- I saw somebody cut-- cut off an ear or break off an ear from somebody and chewing on-- on that flesh.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- at that time-- at that time, I-- I said to myself, if I ever get liberated-- because I had-- the only one that really survived are people that had hope-- if I will ever get enough potato peels in my life, that's all I really would want and want nothing else out of life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yeah, OK.
